summary: In this podcast summary, Dr. Terrie Moffitt discusses findings from the decades-long Dunedin study, emphasizing the distinction between chronological age and the biological 'pace of aging.' She highlights that early-life mental health and self-control are critical predictors of physical health, cognitive decline, and the rate of aging later in life.

principles.statement: Treating mental health issues early in life can prevent chronic diseases and slow biological aging.
principles.rationale: Early-life mental disorders shape physical health decades later; taking mental health seriously serves as a preventative path to a longer, healthier life.

principles.statement: Strong self-control is a key predictor of healthspan and life stability.
principles.rationale: Individuals with strong self-control tend to experience better health, stronger relationships, and greater financial stability as they grow older.

mental_models.name: Pace of Aging vs. Chronological Age
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating human aging that separates the number of years a person has been alive (chronological age) from the biological rate at which their body and mind are declining (pace of aging), which can be tracked via biomarkers.
mental_models.example: Identifying 'fast agers' who show early signs of physical and cognitive decline compared to peers born in the exact same year and place.
